data.title: United Airlines hikes checked bag fee by $10 as fuel prices climbs
data.description:
United Airlines (UAL) hiked its checked bag fee by $10, becoming the second U.S. carrier to raise the fee since the Iran war spiked jet fuel prices.

United’s new fee will cost $45 to check a first bag on most domestic flights if the passenger pays ahead of time, according to the company’s website. If the passenger checks a bag in the airport lobby, fees start at $50, while it will cost $75 at the gate.

The new prices are $10 more than for flights booked before Friday.

"United is raising first and second checked bag fees by $10 for customers traveling in the US, Mexico, and Canada and Latin America beginning with tickets purchased Friday, April 3," the company said in a statement shared with CNBC.

The airline also said on Friday it is rolling out a new tiered fare structure that will offer three options in premium cabins for long-haul international, transcontinental U.S., and select Hawaii flights. The new fare categories would include base, standard, and flexible options for United Polaris and Premium Plus.

The global average jet fuel price last week more than doubled compared to the prior month's average, according to the International Air Transport Association. Jet fuel is the highest expense for airlines after labor costs.

Earlier in the week, JetBlue Airways (JBLU) raised checked bag fees by at least $4 per bag — and up to $9 per bag, depending on when a customer books their travel.

Most U.S. airlines, including United Airlines, JetBlue Airways, and American Airlines, hiked checked baggage fees for customers in 2024 following a rise in maintenance and labor contract costs for carriers.

data.title: Nonfarm payrolls jump past consensus in March, unemployment rate ticks down
data.description:
U.S. nonfarm payrolls in March: +178K vs. +51K consensus and -133K prior (revised from -92K), according to data released by the Bureau of Labor Statistics on Friday.

Unemployment rate: 4.3% vs. 4.4% consensus and 4.4% prior.

Developing… Check back for updates.

data.title: Most and least shorted mid-to mega-cap healthcare stocks in March
data.description:
As the first three months of 2026 end, investors are placing record bets against risky biotech and telehealth startups while flocking to stable pharmaceutical giants for safety. This shift is being driven by higher interest rates, rising energy costs, and new government crackdowns on specific medical services.

Short interest in volatile telehealth and biotech names has hit record levels this March, led by Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) at a staggering 37.93%. Joining it at the top of the bearish list is Tango Therapeutics (TNGX), which currently carries a 33.11% short interest.

data.title: Village Farms announces CFO transition plan as Ruffini moves to lead M&A activities
data.description:
• Village Farms International (VFF) announced that its CFO, Stephen Ruffini, will transition from his role after 17 years to lead the company’s mergers and acquisitions efforts.
  • Ruffini will remain CFO until a permanent successor is appointed, with a formal search process underway.
  • He will continue with the company to ensure a smooth transition while taking on a new leadership role focused on global growth through acquisitions.
  • CEO Michael DeGiglio emphasized Ruffini’s strategic importance and leadership in advancing M&A initiatives.
  • Since joining in 2009, Ruffini has been instrumental in the company’s transformation, including its NASDAQ uplisting, expansion into cannabis, and execution of key financings and transactions.

data.title: Restaurant check: The gas price sell-off may be overdone
data.description:
The restaurant sector has seen large declines since the spike in gas prices stoked concerns that consumers will cut back on discretionary spending in certain categories. Decliners over the last month since the Iran conflict began include Wingstop (WING) -33%, Jack in the Box (JACK) -32%, Venu Holding (VENU) -32%, Dine Brands (DIN) -18%, Bloomin' Brands (BLMN) -16%, Cheesecake Factory (CAKE) -13%, (FWRG) -10%, Chipotle (CMG) -9%, Texas Roadhouse (TXRH) -9%, McDonald's (MCD) -7%, and Starbucks (SBUX) -7%.

However, Mizuho Securities analyst Nick Setyan said that any clear evidence of a slowdown in restaurant trends from higher gas prices is lacking so far. Setyan and his team see the most appealing dislocation within coffee, with Dutch Bros (BROS) still the firm's top pick. Mizuho also continues to view casual dining names as relatively well positioned, with top picks Cheesecake Factory (CAKE) and Brinker International (QSR). Meanwhile, Chipotle (CMG) is the top fast-casual pick. 

If there is continued pressure on consumers due to elevated gas prices, fast-food chains Wendy's (WEN) and Jack in the Box (JACK) are seen by Mizuho as being the most at risk. Baird issued a similar assessment, noting that the lower-income demographic spends a disproportionately high percentage of their income on fuel, meaning that the recent surge in gas prices acts as a direct tax on discretionary food-away-from-home spending, although the double-digit share price declines may be overstating the effect.

The restaurant stocks with the highest Seeking Alpha quant scores are Noodles (NLDS), Brinker International (EAT), Darden Restaurants (DRI), Restaurant Brands International (EAT), and Arcos Dorados (ARCO).
